coredata.dc:title: Excellent optical, flexible, and mechanical properties of chiral cellulose nanocrystal films for structural coloration and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xinyu
Feng, Huimin
Yang, Boying
Ma, Yueer
Li, Xinping
Chang, Hui
Tong, Shuhua
Xiong, Chuanyin
Luo, Yuxia
Zhang, Zhao
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)-based chiral nematic films have recently received attention due to their unique photophysical properties. However, their fragility and single structural color mode (birefringence) restricts their further development. Herein, a flexible optoelectronic, chiral, CNC films was reported composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CNCs produced by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA). Due to weak interactions between CQDs and CNCs, which limit molecular sliding, the optimal tensile strength and curvature reached 65.5 MPa and 2 × 102, respectively. In addition, these products retained not only the structural color of cellulose chiral films in the birefringent mode, but also introduced fluorescence in the CQD ultraviolet excitation mode. Related work also addressed the issue of cellulosic chiral film fragility and provided a new method for their production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystals
Flexible chiral nematic films
Dual mode
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CNC@CQD chiral films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR, XRD and Raman spectra of CQDs (a–c, respectively).

UV–Vis absorption and fluorescent spectrum of CQDs (a) (The solid line is the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, dashed lines are maximum excitation and emission peaks of CQDs) and emission spectra under excitation at different wavelengths (b).

UV–Vis absorption and fluorescent spectrum of CNCs@CQD (a) (The solid line is the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CNCs@CQD, dashed lines are maximum excitation and emission peaks of CNCs@CQD) and emission spectra under excitation by different wavelengths (b).

Transmittance spectrum (a) and emission spectrum (b) of chiral films CNC@CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Transmittance picture of CNC@CQDs films (c) (CQDs content from left to right,0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 100 μL, respectively).
POM spectrum of CNC@CQDs films with different contents of CQDs. CQDs content of 0, 5, 10,15, 20, 25, 30, and 100 μL (a–h from left to right, respectively).
Confocal fluorescence image of CNCs film (a), CNC@CQDs films (b), and pictures of CNC@CQDs film under different angles (c–d).
Plane graph of CNCs film (a), cross-section SEM images of CNCs (b), CNC@CQDs (30 μL) (c), CNC@CQDs (100 μL) (d) films.

Stress-strain curve diagram of CNCs and CNC@CQDs films (a), picture of CNC@CQDs film (b and c), and tensile strength picture of CNCs and CNC@CQDs films (CQDs content of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 100 μL, respectively; d).
Mechanism diagram of CNC@CQDs films tensile performance improvement.

head.ce:title: Excellent optical, flexible, and mechanical properties of chiral cellulose nanocrystal films for structural coloration and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, Shaanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Jinchang Special Paper Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Jinchang Special Paper Co., Ltd. Zhejiang, Quzhou, 324404

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)-based chiral nematic films have recently received attention due to their unique photophysical properties. However, their fragility and single structural color mode (birefringence) restricts their further development. Herein, a flexible optoelectronic, chiral, CNC films was reported composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CNCs produced by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA). Due to weak interactions between CQDs and CNCs, which limit molecular sliding, the optimal tensile strength and curvature reached 65.5 MPa and 2 × 102, respectively. In addition, these products retained not only the structural color of cellulose chiral films in the birefringent mode, but also introduced fluorescence in the CQD ultraviolet excitation mode. Related work also addressed the issue of cellulosic chiral film fragility and provided a new method for their production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, chiral nematic films have been widely used in the fields of optical devices, displays, biomedicine, biosensing, and adaptive optics [1–4]. As a natural polymer material, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) have great prospects due to their high crystallinity, high Young's modulus, large aspect ratio, and good biocompatibility [5,6]. In addition, CNCs form a chiral, nematic, liquid-crystal phase above certain concentrations in aqueous suspensions and, after drying, the helical structure is retained in the solid films as a Bouligand structure. They form a helical structure by interactions of optically active groups between layers, which twist in a regular sequence. The films have unique optical properties, such as large rotational light, selective reflection, and special texture images in polarized light. The same position at different angles will produce different structural color due to Bragg reflection [7,8]. Thus, the reflection wavelength can be changed through the regulation of structure and pitch, thus forming colorful optical materials, which already have broad application prospects in the fields of anti-counterfeiting [9].

Chiral nematic films cannot meet practical applications in high-grade anticounterfeiting materials owing to their poor mechanical properties and single structural color anti-counterfeiting channel. As is known, the chiral nematic films usually exist in the form of short rod-like nanofibers, which form regular and ordered rigid structures through evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) between cellulose instead of deposited chains, resulting in poor mechanical properties. Wu et al. [10] have modified CNCs using succinic anhydride (SCNCs) and then melt-blended SCNCs with polybutylene succinate (PBS) to prepare nanocomposites. These nanocomposites have been demonstrated to possess positive thermal stability and mechanical properties, in which the optimal tensile strength can reach 39 MPa. Also, to improve the anti-counterfeiting performance of CNCs film, Shojaeiarani et al. [11] have prepared CNC/zinc sulfide/polylactic acid (CNC/ZnS/PLA) nanocomposite films via a film casting method. It was shown that CNCs loaded with ZnS quantum dots have effective fluorescence. However, as ZnS nanoparticles (NPs) tend to cause stress concentration, the mechanical properties are weak, making it difficult to improve flexibility. In recent years, CQDs have been sown with the advantages of stable fluorescence performance, hydrophilicity, excitation wavelength correlation, and good water solubility [12–14]. For example, Gan et al. [15] combined carbon quantum dots (CDs) and CNCs to form ultraviolet (UV) filtration membranes. Studies have shown that the film has strong fluorescence and mechanical properties. With the strong combination of hydrogen bonds and cross-linking between dense CNC and UV resin, the tensile strength is 10.78 MPa. Owing to the reduction of intermolecular sliding, their mechanical properties are reduced. In addition, the insertion of chemical bonds affected the structure of the cellulose nanocrystals, leading to the disappearance of chiral nematic structure.

In this study, ethylenediamine (EDA) was used as a nitrogen source and citric acid (CA) as carbon source to hydrothermally synthesize CQDs. CNC@CQD films were fabricated by co-assembling CQDs with CNCs via EISA. A detailed structural study of CNC@CQD chiral films was performed. The photophysical properties of the composites were also analyzed and characterized and found to be regulated by the CQD concentration, providing structural colors and fluorescence dual-channel anticounterfeiting potential. The optimal tensile strength reached 65.5 MPa and curvature 2 × 102, which provided a new method for solving the fragility problem of CNCs.

CNCs were purchased from Rui Niu Energy New Material Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). CA (≥99.5%) and EDA (≥99.5%) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All solutions were prepared with deionized water.

Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex 70 IR spectrometer (Bruker Corp., Billerica, MA, USA) in the spectral range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 using pressed potassium bromide discs. The material was coated with gold to a thickness of ∼8 nm and then the fracture surfaces of samples examined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM; S-4800, Hitachi Instruments, Inc., Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 3.0 kV. X-ray diffractograms (XRD) were obtained with a Nihon Reiji Smart diffractometer (Rigaku Corp., Tokyo, Japan), using Cu-Kα radiation and the 2ϴ from 5 to 60o. Structure was recorded by Raman spectroscopy (AVANCE NEO 600 MHz, Bruker Corp.). UV–Vis and fluorescent spectra were recorded using an UV-VIS-NIR spectrometer (Carry 5000 UV, Agilent Technologies, Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA), a fluorescence spectrometer (FS 5, Edinburgh, UK), respectively. The liquid crystallinity and structure were recorded using a Primotech polarizing light microscope (POM; Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, Germany). Thermal stability curves were recorded using a STA449F-1053-M (Netzsch Instruments North America, Burlington, MA, USA) simultaneous thermal analyzer (TG), Germany, using a 2 × 2 cm size paper fixed on a slide. The laser confocal lens selected to the most suitable excitation wavelength at 400 nm for analysis. The paper was cut into 1 cm width strips and fixed between two ends of a clamp, with the parameters set for mechanical property testing. The thickness of chiral thin films was measured using a paper thickness meter (DC-HJY03, Sichuan, China; working precision is 0.001 mm).

Referring by Huang et al. [16] for the synthesis of CQDs: 1.5 g of CA were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water with 2 mL of EDA. After 30 min of sonication, the samples were transferred to the reaction reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. Then, filtering the crude product, a brown-yellow solution was obtained. The solution was centrifuged (1000 rmp) for 10 min and the supernatant dialyzed in a dialysis bag (300 Da) for 3 d, with the water changed every 7 h. Finally, a solution of CQDs was obtained, which were sealed and stored in a refrigerator for later use (the quality concentration of CQDs is 0.1 wt%).
Preparation of CNCs with CQDs (CNC@CQDs) and chiral cellulose nanocrystal films of CQDs (CNC@CQDs films)

CQDs of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 50, 100 μL were added to 16 mL of 1.25% CNCs, stirred and sonicated for 2 h to obtain CNC@CQD. Finally, this product was poured into petri dishes and formed into films through EISA for 7 d. According to different CQD contents in composite materials, samples were labeled as CNC@CQDs films (5 μL), CNC@CQDs films (10 μL), CNC@CQDs films (15 μL), CNC@CQDs films (20 μL), CNC@CQDs films (25 μL), CNC@CQDs films (30 μL), CNC@CQDs films (50 μL), and CNC@CQDs films (100 μL), respectively. (The thickness of the composite films are 0.030⁓0.035 mm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CNC@CQD chiral films
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared utilizing a hydrothermal reaction with EDA and CA together for 4 h at 180 °C before filtering and further purification. The liquid suspension of CNC@CQD was prepared by ultrasound treatment and repeatedly combining various proportions of CQDs and CNCs. The liquid suspension was then added in petri dishes and chiral films of CNC@CQDs created by EISA (Scheme 1 ).

The structure of CQDs was examined by FT-IR analyses (Fig. 1 a). The results showed that the absorption peaks of CQDs at 1048 cm−1 were caused by in-plane bending vibrations of O–H, with peaks at 1640 and 1567 cm−1 indicating the presence of CO and CC/N–H [17,18]. The broad absorption peak at 3441 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups, the peak at 3260 cm−1 implied the presence of the functional group N–H, and the peak at 2978 cm−1 caused by C–H. Moreover, from the XRD spectra of CQDs, a broad diffraction peak was observed at 22.6, which indicated that the CQDs were amorphous structures (Fig. 1b). The sharp peak indicated that CQD conformation was graphitic crystal structures, corresponding to (002) planes of CQDs, which thus indicated that CQDs with amorphous structure and crystalline carbon core had been successfully synthesized [19]. Raman images showed that the CQDs exhibited two peaks at 1360 (D-band) and 1580 cm−1 (G-bands, Fig. 1c). The intensity ratio of D and G (
I D
/
I G
) of these CQDs was <1, indicating a good degree of graphitization [20].

Combining CQDs with CNCs in an aqueous phase resulted in the introduction of N–H and the peak at 1567 cm−1 of CNC@CQDs film was related to CQD content (Fig. S1). Also, the XRD spectra confirmed that CQDs did not affect the structure of nanocellulose films (Fig. S2). The peaks at 15.2, 16.7, and 22.8° for chiral films corresponded to the crystal planes (110), (110), and (200), which exhibited no clear change with the diffraction peaks of CNC@CQDs film. Meanwhile, the crystallinity of CNCs and CNC@CQDs films were calculated to be 92 and 89, respectively, according to the crystallinity formula:
CrI =
(
I cr
−
I am
)
/
I cr
× 100
[21].

The thermal stability of CNCs and CNC@CQDs (30 μL) fluorescent paper from room temperature to 800 °C was analyzed by TG and DTG tests (Fig. S3-S4). With increased temperature, CNC@CQDs film began to lose weight to a small extent and the weight loss reaching 3.07%, which was due to dehydration and carbonization of functional groups on CQD surfaces. After heating to 210 °C, the rate of weight loss started to increase, caused by the breakdown of amino groups. The rapid weight loss of CNCs film started at 180 °C and weight loss of CNCs film with CQDs was decreased compared with that of CNCs film, indicating that the thermal stability of CNCs film had been improved by CQD addition [22]. After 335 °C, the weight loss decrease of CNC@CQDs film was more than that of CNCs film, which might have been caused by continued decomposition of amino groups on CQDs. Moreover, CNCs and CNC@CQDs films showed similar mass decrease trends. At 800 °C, the lost weights of CNCs and the CNC@CQDs films were 49.69% and 47.90%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of dispersion of CQDs and CNC@CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CQDs and corresponding optical photos showed that the absorption peak at 238 nm was due to π-π* transitions of CC, while the peak at 338 nm were n-π* transitions of CO and CN double bonds (Fig. 2 a) [23]. The maximum excitation and emission peaks of CQDs were caused by an absorption peak at 338 nm, while the emission peak at 445 nm was n-π* transitions of CO and CN double bonds. When the excitation wavelength moved to a longer wavelength, the emission wavelength also moved to a longer wavelength, which meant that the emission wavelength of CQDs depended on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b, inner inset shows CQDs as pale yellow in daylight and blue in UV irradiation at 365 nm).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNCs@CQD dispersions showed that absorption peaks at 240 and 340 nm corresponded to π-π* electron and n-π* electron transitions of CC and CO bonds of CQDs, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The maximum excitation wavelength of 355 nm was close to the UV absorption peak, indicating that the emission peak at 446 nm could be attributed to n-π* transitions of CO and CN [24]. There was a slight redshift of the simultaneous absorption peak after CQDs introduced to CNCs. The emission peak varied with the excitation wavelength, which indicated that the dispersion was excitation dependent (Fig. 3b). Under natural light, the dispersions appeared pale yellow, while they appeared blue under UV excitation at 365 nm (insert of Fig. 3a). Under the same conditions, CNCs@CQD can emit bright blue light, mainly thanks to the emission of CQDs center. CNCs and CQDs are prone to weak interactions, which reduced the quenching effect induced by high concentration aggregations of CQDs, thus resulting in a brighter fluorescence effect.

The transmission spectrum of CNC@CQDs films with different CQDs content showed that CNC film had a trough at 530 nm, which meant that CNC film had the ability to reflect 530 nm light, such that the CNC@CQDs films reflected the color phenomenon (Fig. 4 a). This observation showed that the photon band of CNC@CQDs films had changed significantly as CQDs content increased. When CQDs content increased to 30 μL, the peak and valley of CNC@CQDs films shifted toward blue, from 530 to 335 nm. Compared with pure CNCs chiral films, the wave troughs of chiral CNC@CQDs films were more significant with increased CQDs content. The transmittance increased to 85.7% with increased CQDs content. At a concentration of 100 μL, the trough of the chiral films was at 340 nm, corresponding to the absorption peak of CQDs at 338 nm, which was due to π-π* transitions. In addition, the position of the trough did not change as CQDs content increased. The physical transmission diagram showed that, with increased CQDs, from 0 to 30 μL, the transmittance of the films gradually became clear, which was mainly due to light scattering (Fig. 4c; [25]). However, when the concentration increased to 100 μL, although the picture was clear, the structural color of the film could not be observed. The film's transmission spectral minimum at 0–25 μL CQDs was too faint and too broad, indicating that the selective reflection was inadequate. There was no minimum transmission value attributable to selective reflection for CQDs of 30 μL or above and the minimum transmission value at 350 nm induced by CQDs. There was no minimum transmission in the visible range for 30 μL CQDs films. Therefore, an appropriate amount of CQDs increased the transmittance of nanocellulose film, but excessive CQDs will cause the film to lose its structural color.

To further study the photoluminescence characteristics of CNC@CQDs films, the emission spectra of the chiral films, with CQDs content at 10, 20, 25, 30, and 100 μL, at 400 nm excitation were examined (Fig. 4b). The emission wavelengths were observed to be 553, 573, 526, 518, and 560 nm, respectively. With the emission wavelengths of these CNC@CQDs redshifted compared to CQDs (450 nm), which was caused by interactions between CNCs and CQDs [26]. CNC@CQDs film (30 μL) showed the strongest fluorescence, while CNC@CQDs film (100 μL) showed a reduction, which was due to fluorescence quenching caused by high CQDs concentration. These chiral films emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S5). The fluorescence intensity became more clear as the CQDs concentration increased to 30 μL, but ceased to be enhanced when the concentration reached 100 μL.

SEM images of CNCs and CNC@CQDs films with different CQDs contents showed that CNCs films surfaces were smooth, with fibers grouped densely (Fig. 7 ). Compared to the pitch length of pure material, the pitch length increase was caused by the presence of additional CQDs. At the same time, CNCs spiral shapes gave these films a twofold refractive effect that allowed them to reflect blue light in ambient light. Moreover, the cross-section of CNC@CQDs film (30 μL) showed that the chiral film retained a helical structure while forming inside fiber bridges (Fig. 7c). The addition of 30 μL of CQDs was partially retained the chiral helical structure of the film. Due to the self-assembly of the chiral nematic phase, the alternating “hills” and “valleys” in the cross-section correspond to the CNCs parallel and perpendicular to the cross-section [29]. The CNCs exhibit a right-handed chiral convoluted morphology after layer-by-layer stacking, providing the chiral center for the CNC films to form a left-handed chiral nematic phase, and leaving chiral nematic phase [30]. However, it was worth noting that the original nanocellulose pitch could hardly be seen in chiral film with 100 μL CQDs content, indicating that the introduction of excessive CQDs could destroy the chiral nematic structure of nanocellulose film (Fig. 7d).

In summary, low-cost, nontoxic, luminescent CQDs were designed to improve the fragility of nanocellulose films. Compared with the previous coupling between CNCs and CQDs, the study found that through the chiral CNCs films, an appropriate amount of CQDs can improve the mechanical properties and elasticity of the chiral nematic film. Due to weak interactions between CQDs and CNCs, which limit molecular sliding, the optimal tensile strength and curvature reached 65.5 MPa and 2 × 102, respectively. In addition, these products retained not only the structural color of cellulose chiral films in the birefringent mode, but also introduced fluorescence in the CQDs ultraviolet excitation mode. The CNCs-based chiral nematic films with excellent flexibility, mechanical properties, structural coloring, and fluorescence properties have potential applications in anti-counterfeiting.
All authors consent to the publication of this manuscript.

I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have consulted the Guide for Authors in preparing our manuscript. No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and manuscript was approved by all authors for publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171170, 21703131, 22078184, 12004232), Doctoral Start-up Fund of Shaanxi University of Science and Technology (2019BJ-34), Shaanxi Provincial Key R & D Plan (2022GY-279).
